question: The position of $$\mathrm{Br}$$ in the compound, $$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHC}(\mathrm{Br})\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2}$$ can be classified as____.
explanation:
Allyl halides are those compounds in which the halogen atom is bonded to ${\rm{s}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{3}}}$ hybridised carbon atom next to carbon, carbondouble bond.
e.g., $$\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{X}$$ and
$$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHC}(\mathrm{Br})\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2}$$.
Aryl halides are the compounds in which the halogen atom is bonded to the ${\rm{s}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{2}}}$ hybridised carbon atom of an aromatic ring. e.g., https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954288/preptracker/questions/q_26033_explanation_1764954288439.jpg Vinyl halides are the compounds in which the halogen atom is bonded to the $$s p^{2}$$ hybridised carbon atom of a carbon carbon-double bond.
e.g., $$\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{X}$$
Secondary alkyl halides are the compounds in which the halogen atom is bonded to the $$s p^{2}$$ hybridised carbon atom which is further bonded to two alkyl groups and one hydrogen atom. e.g., https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954289/preptracker/questions/q_26033_explanation_1764954289216.jpg

question:
Find the reactants which on heating with alcoholic KOH produces the compound $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$.
(I) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958209/preptracker/questions/q_107415_question_1764958209254.png
(II) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958210/preptracker/questions/q_107415_question_1764958209839.png
(III) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958210/preptracker/questions/q_107415_question_1764958210441.png
(IV) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958211/preptracker/questions/q_107415_question_1764958211115.png
explanation:
On heating haloalkane with alcoholic KOH , gives $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ are : (I) ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - {\text{CH}} - \mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{{\text{Br}}} } {{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}$ $\xrightarrow[{{\text{Elimination}}}]{{{\text{KOH}}\,\,({\text{alc}}.)}}$ ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{CH}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}$
Hence, the formation of given product is possible, but in minor quantity. (IV) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958212/preptracker/questions/q_107415_explanation_1764958212041.png
Hence, the formation of given product is possible. Thus, (I) and (IV) are the reactants that can give the given product.
Hence, option (2) is the correct answer.

question: 15.7 g of 1-chloropropane is treated with alcoholic KOH . What mass of propene (in grams) is obtained if the yield is ${\mathrm{50 \%}}$ ?
explanation:
When 1-chloropropane is heated with alcoholic KOH , propene is obtained.$\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{Cl}}}\limits_{{\text{1 - Chloropropane}}} {\text{ + }}\mathop {{\text{KOH}}}\limits_{\left( {{\text{Alc}}{\text{.}}} \right)} \xrightarrow{\Delta }$
$\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{CH}}}\limits_{{\text{Propane}}} {\text{ = C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + KCl + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$
Molar mass of ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{Cl}} = {\text{78}}{\text{.5}}\,{\text{g/mol}}$ and molar mass of propene ${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}\right)=42 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ Number of moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}=\dfrac{15.7 \mathrm{~g}}{78.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}=0.2 \mathrm{~mol}}}$
0.2 mol of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}}}$ will give 0.2 mol of propene.
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ Mass of propene obtained will be ${\mathrm{0.2 \times 42=8.4 \mathrm{~g}}}$
However, the yield is ${\mathrm{50 \%}}$.
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ Mass of propene obtained ${\mathrm{=8.4 \times \dfrac{50}{100}=4.2 \mathrm{~g}}}$

question:
Assertion: Tertiary haloalkanes are more reactive than primary haloalkanes towards elimination reactions.


Reason: The $+\mathrm{I}$-effect of the alkyl groups weakenss the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond.
explanation: Tertiary haloalkanes exhibit a preference for elimination reactions over substitution due to steric hindrance. $+\mathrm{I}$ effect weakens the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond is true. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Alkyl halides form alkenes when heated above $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.


Reason: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{I}$ reacts slowly with strong base as compared to $\mathrm{CD}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{I}$.
explanation: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{I}$ reacts more rapidly with strong base in comparison to $\mathrm{CD}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{I}$. The elimination of $\mathrm{HI}$ (or DI) in presence of strong base shows $E 2$ elimination. The rate determining step involves the breaking up of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ ( or $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{D}$ ) bond, The $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{D}$ bond being stronger than $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954448/preptracker/questions/q_26690_explanation_1764954447580.png  So option (3) is correct.

2-Methylbut-2-ene as major product.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{HCHO}$

(A) $\rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}$

question: n-propyl bromide on treating with alcoholic $$\mathrm{KOH}$$ produces
explanation:
$\begin{array}{l}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{Br + KOH}} \to \\{\rm{Propylbromide}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(alc)}}\end{array}$
$\begin{array}{l} {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{CH = C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + KBr + }}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}\\\,\,\,{\rm{Propane}}\end{array}$
This reaction removes a molecule of $$\mathrm{HBr}$$ and therefore, the reaction is called dehydrohalogenation. The hydrogen atom is eliminated from $$\beta$$ carbon atom (carbon atom next to the carbon to which halogen is attached). Therefore the reaction is also called $$\beta$$ elimination reaction. Alc. $$\mathrm{KOH}$$ is a good dehydrohalogenating agent.

question:
Elimination reaction of 2-Bromo-pentane to form pent-2-ene is:
(a) $$\beta$$-Elimination reaction
(b) Follows Zaitsev rule
(c) Dehydrohalogenation reaction
(d) Dehydration reaction
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956711/preptracker/questions/q_26216_explanation_1764956711016.png Sec.alkylhaide
This reaction is an example of $$\beta$$-elimination
Hydrogen is removed from $$\beta$$-carbon and halogen from $$\alpha$$-carbon, hence dehydrohalgoenation reaction. Generally in $$\mathrm{E}_{2}$$ reaction Zaitsev alkene is formed as major product (more stable alkene).

question:
Consider the following alkyl halides
$\mathop {{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{5}}}{\rm{I,}}}\limits_{\rm{I}} \,\,\,\mathop {{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{5}}}{\rm{Cl}}}\limits_{{\rm{II}}} {\rm{,}}\,\,\,\,\mathop {{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{5}}}{\rm{Br}}}\limits_{{\rm{III}}} {\rm{,}}\,\,\,\mathop {\,{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{5}}}{\rm{\,F}}}\limits_{{\rm{IV}}} $
(I) I, undergoes faster dehydrohalogenation than (II) and (IV).
(II) II, undergoes lower dehydrohalogenation than (I).
(III) IV, has least ability to undergo dehydrohalogenation.
Choose the correct option.
explanation: The reactivity order or alkyl halides towards dehydrohalogenation can be explained on the basis of $$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$$ bond strength. Lower the bond strength, higher is the reactivity. Hence, order is $$\mathrm{I}>\mathrm{III}>\mathrm{II}>\mathrm{IV}$$

question: Elimination reactions of cis- and trans-1-bromo-2-methylcyclohexanes with $$\mathrm{NaOEt}$$ in $$\mathrm{EtOH}$$ can give the same or different main products, 1 methylcyclohexene (A) or 3-methylcyclohexene (B). Which statement is true?
explanation: The substituents at the 1,2 positions of a chair cyclohexane take an axial and an equatorial position in the cis form. But both take equatorial positions or both at axial positions in the trans form. Consequently, the axial $$\mathrm{Br}$$ has an adjacent axial hydrogen at $$\mathrm{C}_{2}$$ (and $$\mathrm{C}_{6}$$ in the cis form but only at $$\mathrm{C}_{6}$$ in the trans form. Consequently, the former can give the more substituted one but the latter cannot. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953363/preptracker/questions/q_25579_explanation_1764953363160.jpg

options.text: A, both from cis and trans substrates
B, both from cis and trans substrates
A from cis and B from trans substrate
B from cis and A from trans substrate

question:
The number of optical isomers of the compound,
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{Br})-\mathrm{COOH}$ is
explanation:
Number of optical isomers $=2^{\mathrm{n}}$ (when the molecule has no symmetry).
Where, ${\rm{n = }}$ no.of chiral carbon atoms.
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - \mathop {\text{C}}\limits^* {\text{H}}({\text{Br}}) - {\text{COOH}}$
No.of chiral carbons $=1$
$\therefore$ No. of optical isomers are $=2^{\mathrm{n}}=2^{1}=2$

question: Match the methods of preparation of alkyl halides in Column I with their corresponding descriptions or outcomes in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
(A) Alcohols, when reacted with concentrated halogen acids like $\mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{HBr}$, or HI , or phosphorus halides such as $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}, \mathrm{PBr}_{3}$, or $\mathrm{PI}_{3}$, result in the substitution of the hydroxyl group with a halogen, forming haloalkanes (S).
(B) Alkanes undergo free radical halogenation when treated with chlorine or bromine in the presence of light or heat, leading to the formation of haloalkanes (R).
(C) Alkenes react with hydrogen halides (HX where X is $\mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{Br}$, or I) to form alkyl halides. The reaction follows Markovnikov's rule, which states that the hydrogen from the hydrogen halide adds to the carbon with the most hydrogen atoms (Q).
(D) The Halogen Exchange, also known as the Finkelstein reaction, is when alkyl chlorides or bromides react with sodium iodide (NaI) in dry acetone, forming alkyl iodides with the precipitation of NaCl or $\mathrm{NaBr}(\mathrm{P})$.
Correct option is (1)

options.text: A - S,        	B - R, 	C - Q, 	D - P
A - Q, 	B - R,	C - S,	D - P
A - R, 	B - S,	C - P, 	D - Q
A - S,  	B - P,	C - Q, 	D - R

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Miscellaneous questions on methods of preparation of haloalknes

question:
The products ${\mathrm{A}}$ and ${\mathrm{B}}$ obtained in the following reactions, respectively, are
${\rm{3ROH + PC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{3}}} \to {\rm{3RCl + A}}$
${\rm{ROH + PC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{5}}} \to {\rm{RCl + HCl + B}}$
explanation:
$\begin{aligned}& 3 \mathrm{ROH}+\mathrm{PCl}_{3} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{RCl}+\underset{(\mathrm{A})}{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{3}} \\& \mathrm{ROH}+\mathrm{PCl}_{5} \rightarrow \mathrm{RCl}+\mathrm{HCl}+\underset{(\mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{POCl}}\end{aligned}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \mathrm{A}}}$ and B are ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{3}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{POCl}_{3}}}$ respectively.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: Alkyl iodide can be prepared by treating alkyl chloride/bromide with $\mathrm{NaI}$ in acetone.


Reason: $\mathrm{NaCl} / \mathrm{NaBr}$ are soluble in acetone while $\mathrm{NaI}$ is not.
explanation: This is Finkelstein reaction which involves the conversion of an alkyl chloride/bromide to an alkyl iodide by the addition of sodium iodide in acetone. Because sodium iodide is soluble in acetone and $\mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{NaBr}$ are not, the equilibrium is shifted by the precipitation of insoluble salt. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Free radical chlorination of n-butane gives $72 \%$ of 2 -chlorobutane and $28 \%$ of 1 chlorobutane though it has six primary and four secondary hydrogens.


Reason: A secondary hydrogen is abstracted more easily than the primary hydrogen.
explanation: Selective abstraction of secondary hydrogens in n-butane during free radical chlorination leads to a higher yield of 2-chlorobutane, favoring the formation of the more stable secondary radicals. Therefore option (1) is correct.

Assertion: Hydrogen iodide reacts readily with alkenes than hydrogen fluoride.


Reason: Aqueous hydrohalogen acids are used to prepare alkyl halides from alkenes.
explanation: Reactivity order $\mathrm{HI}>\mathrm{HBr}>\mathrm{HCl}>\mathrm{HF}$, based on bond energies. Dry gaseous hydrohalogen acids are better electrophiles. Also in aqueous solution, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, acting as nucleophile may produce alcohol. Thus, assertion is correct but reason is false and option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Alkyl iodides darken on standing.

Reason: Alkyl iodides are prepared by Finkelstein reaction.
explanation: Less stable iodides tend to lose two iodine atoms. These liberated iodine atoms (2 I) are then absorbed by other iodides, causing a darkening in their color. Alkyl iodides are indeed prepared by Finkelstein reaction. But it is not the correct explanation for the assertion. So option (2) is correct.

explanation:

question:

$\mathop {{\rm{2R - Cl}}}\limits_{{\rm{Alkylchloride}}} {\rm{ + Co}}{{\rm{F}}_{\rm{2}}} \to \mathop {{\rm{2R - F}}}\limits_{{\rm{ Alkyl fluoride }}} {\rm{ + CoC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}$
When alkyl chloride or alkyl bromide is heated with metallic fluoride $$\left(\mathrm{CoF}_{2}\right)$$ then alkyl fluoride and corresponding metal chloride are produced.

question: Which among the following methods is not suitable for the preparation of alkyl chlorides?
explanation:
Chlorination of alkanes in presence of sunlight is not suitable for the preparation of alkyl chloride because chlorination of alkanes gives a complex mixture of isomeric mono and polyhaloalkanes, which is difficult to separate as pure compounds. Consequently, the yield of any one compound is low.
$\begin{array}{*{20}{r}}{{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{/UVlight}}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{Cl}}} \\ {{\text{ + C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{CHClC}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \end{array}$

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: Aryl chlorides and bromides can be prepared by electrophilic substitution of arenes with chlorine and bromine respectively in the presence of Lewis acid catalysts like iron or iron (III) chloride.

Reason: Lewis acid catalysts like iron or iron (III) chloride facilitate the generation of the more reactive halogen species by forming a complex with chlorine or bromine.
explanation: The Assertion is true because the preparation of aryl chlorides and bromides does indeed involve the electrophilic substitution of arenes, which is facilitated by Lewis acid catalysts that make the halogens more reactive. The Reason is also true and is the correct explanation for the Assertion, because the Lewis acid catalysts, like iron or iron(III) chloride, form a complex with halogens, which makes them better electrophiles that can successfully attack the aromatic ring. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Assertion is false, but Reason is true.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Different Preparation Methods from Aromatic Compounds

question:
Given below are two statements :
Benzenediazonium salt is prepared by the reaction of aniline with nitrous acid at $273-278 \mathrm{~K}$. It decomposes easily in the dry state.
Insertion of iodine into the benzene ring is difficult and hence iodobenzene is prepared through the reaction of benzenediazonium salt with KI.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
explanation:
Benzene diazonium chloride is prepared by the reaction of aniline with nitrous acid at $273-278 \mathrm{~K}$.
${{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5}{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2} + {\text{NaN}}{{\text{O}}_2} + 2{\text{HCl}}\xrightarrow{{273 - 278\,{\text{K}}}}$
${{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5}\mathop {{{\text{N}}_2}}\limits^ + \mathop {{\text{Cl}}}\limits^ - + {\text{NaCl}} + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$
Benzene diazonium chloride decomposes easily in the dry state.
Iodobenzene is prepared by shaking benzene diazonium salt with KI because direct insertion of iodine into benzene ring is difficult
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952149/preptracker/questions/q_107409_explanation_1764952149086.png
Thus, both Statement A and Statement B are correct

options.text: Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct
Both Statement A and Statement B are correct
Both Statement A and Statement B are incorrect
Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect

question:
Following statements are given regarding the preparation of arylhalides from toluene. Read the following statements and choose the correct option.
(i) Aryl chlorides and bromides can be easily prepared by this method.
(ii) The ortho and para isomers formed in the reaction can not be separated easily due to small difference in their melting point.
(iii) Reactions with iodine are reversible in nature and require the presence of an oxidising agent.
(iv) Fluoro compounds are not prepared by this method due to low reactivity of fluorine.
explanation:
Aryl chlorides and bromides can be easily prepared by electrophilic substitution of arenes with chlorine and bromine respectively in the presence of Lewis acid catalysts like iron or iron (III) chloride.
The ortho and para isomers can be easily separated due to large difference in their melting points.
Reaction with iodine are reversible in nature and require the presence of an oxidising agent $$\left(\mathrm{HNO}_{3}, \mathrm{HIO}_{4}\right)$$ to oxidise the $$\mathrm{HI}$$ formed during iodination.
Fluoro compounds are not prepared by this method due to high reactivity of fluorine.

${\rm{A}} - {\rm{S}}$ ; $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CHBr}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{KOH} / \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}}$ Alkenyl bromide.
$\because \mathrm{KOH}$ (alc.) will remove one H and one Br to give alkenyl bromide.
${\rm{B}} - {\rm{P}}$ ; $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} \xrightarrow[\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CO}\right)_{2}, \Delta]{\mathrm{HBr}}$
$1^{\circ}$ alkyl bromide is formed due to presence of peroxide and HBr (Anti - Markovnikov's addition).
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Q} ; \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Br}_{2}, \mathrm{hv}}$
$2^{\circ}$ alkyl bromide is formed because $2^{\circ}$ free radical is more stable.
$\mathrm{D}-\mathrm{R} ; \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{NBS}}$
allyl bromide is formed due to presence of NBS.
Hence, option (4) is the correct answer.

options.text: A - P,     B - S, 	C - Q,    D - R

$\mathop {{\text{2}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{Cl}}}\limits_{\mathop {{\text{n - Propyl}}\,{\text{chloride}}}\limits_{{\text{(1 - Chloropropane)}}} } + \mathop {{\text{A}}{{\text{g}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}}\limits_{\mathop {{\text{Dry}}}\limits_{{\text{silver}}\,{\text{oxide}}} } \,\xrightarrow{\Delta }\,\,\,$

From above reaction, we say that 2 moles of n-propyl chloride is required.
So, the correct option is (2).

question: The compound having longest $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond is
explanation: In chlorobenzene, 1-chloro-4 -nitrobenzene and vinyl chloride, formation of double bond takes place between $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond due to resonance but in 4-chlorocyclohexene, no such resonance takes place, hence, $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond length is longest in this compound.  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958273/preptracker/questions/q_107547_explanation_1764958272648.png

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The bond enthalpies of the C-X bonds decrease in the order of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}> \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{I}$.
This is due to the increasing size and decreasing electronegativity of the halogen atoms as we move from fluorine to iodine in the periodic table.
explanation: Statement A  is correct; the bond enthalpies decrease in the order given because the bonds become longer and weaker as the size of the halogen increases. Statement B correctly explains the trend, as the decreasing bond strength is indeed due to the increasing size and decreasing electronegativity of the halogen atoms down the group. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
In haloalkanes, the carbon atom bearing the halogen atom bears a partial positive charge.
Haloalkanes are more reactive towards nucleophilic substitution reactions than haloarenes because the carbon-halogen bond is less polar in haloarenes.
explanation: Statement  A  is true because halogens are more electronegative than carbon, resulting in a polarized carbon-halogen bond with the carbon having a partial positive charge. Statement B is also true as the delocalization of the lone pair of electrons on the halogen into the aromatic system in haloarenes reduces the polarity of the carbon-halogen bond, making them less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution reactions. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The ${\text{C}} - {\text{I}}$ bond length is greater than the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Br}$ bond length in haloalkanes.

Reason: As we move down the group in the periodic table from bromine to iodine, the size of the halogen atom increases, leading to an increase in bond length.
explanation: The size of the iodine atom is larger than that of the bromine atom, which results in a longer bond length for $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{I}$ compared to $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Br}$. This is consistent with the periodic trends in atomic size. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Assertion is false, but Reason is true.

subject: Chemistry

question: Among the following, which one has the weakest carbon-halogen bond?
explanation: ${\rm{C}} - {\rm{X}}$ bond in benzyl bromide is much weaker than in vinyl bromide and bromobenzene since the benzyl cation left after the removal of the bromide ion is stabilised by resonance. Further, $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Br}$ bond is weaker than $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond due to larger size of $-\mathrm{Br}$. Therefore, $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}$ has the weakest ${\rm{C}} - {\rm{X}}$ bond.

The compound $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{~F}$ would be named as "3-Fluoropropane."
explanation: The correct IUPAC name would be "1 - Fluoropropane" as the carbon chain numbering should give the halogen the lowest possible number. The carbon chain is numbered from the end nearest a substituent to give it the lowest number. Therefore, the correct name for $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{~F}$ is "1-Fluoropropane", not "3- Fluoropropane". Correct option is (2)

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Benzyl halides are more reactive than vinyl halides in nucleophilic substitution reactions.
The $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridization of the carbon atom in benzyl halides makes the ${\text{C}} - {\text{X}}$ bond stronger and less reactive than in vinyl halides.
explanation: Statement  A  is true; benzyl halides are indeed more reactive because the benzyl cation is stabilized by resonance. Statement B is false because the $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridization actually makes the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond weaker in benzyl halides compared to the $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridized carbon of vinyl halides,leading to increased reactivity. Correct option is (2)

explanation: Statement A is true because, in an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ mechanism, the nucleophile indeed attacks from the side opposite to the leaving group (backside attack), which results in an inversion of configuration at the carbon center, making Statement B false. Correct option is (2)

question:

Assertion: Alkyl halides react with silver nitrite to form nitroalkanes.

Reason: In alkyl halides, the carbon-halogen bond is polar, which facilitates the nucleophilic substitution reaction with silver nitrite to form nitroalkanes.
explanation: The Assertion is true as alkyl halides can react with silver nitrite to form nitroalkanes. However, the Reason is false because the reaction with silver nitrite actually leads to the formation of nitroalkanes, not alkyl nitrites. It is the nature of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{2}$ which is the actual reason for the formation of nitroalkanes upon reaction of alkyl halide rather than the nature of alkyl halide in $\mathrm{Ag}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O}, \mathrm{Ag}-\mathrm{O}$ bond is covalent nature hence nucleophilic attack takes place by N atom's lone pair electrons. Hence leads to formation of nitro alkanes. Correct option is (3).

Species (ii) shows aromatic nature along with resonance, thus gives most stable carbocation.
Species (i) shows less stable carbocation due to absence of aromatic nature, it only shows resonance effect.
Species (iii) is least stable because it is secondary carbocation which is stabilised by hyper conjugation. Hence, the correct order is ii > i > iii

question:
The order of reactivity of the following compounds towards dilute aqueous KOH in $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reaction is
I. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}$
II. $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{CCH}_{2} \mathrm{CHBrCH}_{3}$
III. $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right) \mathrm{CHCH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}$
IV. $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{CBr}$
explanation:
The ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}{\rm{1}}$ reaction proceeds by the formation of carbonium ion. The order of stability of carbonium ion is, $3^{\circ}>2^{\circ}>1^{\circ}$
Also, more the number of carbon atoms, more will be the stability of carbonium ion. Therefore, the correct order of reactivity of $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reaction is, ${\text{I}} < {\text{III}} < {\text{II}} < {\text{IV }}{\text{.}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_3} - {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_2} - {\rm{Br}} + {\rm{N}}{{\rm{u}}^ \ominus } \to $ ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_3} - {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_2} - {\rm{Nu}} + {\rm{B}}{{\rm{r}}^ \ominus }$ The decreasing order of the reaction rate with nucleophile ( $\mathrm{Nu}^{\ominus}$ ) is $\mathrm{Nu}^{\ominus}=$
(I) $\mathrm{PhO}^{\ominus}$;
(II) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{\ominus}$;
(III) $\mathrm{OH}^{\ominus}$;
(IV) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{\ominus}$
explanation:
Hydroxide ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$ is stronger nucleophile than acetate ion as in acetate ion, the negative charge is involved in resonance with carboxylic group. Methoxide ion $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{-}\right)$ is more nucleophilic than hydroxide ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$ due to +I effect of methyl group.
$\mathrm{PhO}^{\ominus}$ is very weak nucleophile because the negative charge is in resonance with ring and charge further decreases. The order is $\underset{\text { (IV) }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{\ominus}}>\underset{\text { (III) }}{\mathrm{OH}^{\ominus}}>\underset{\text { (I) }}{\mathrm{PhO}^{\ominus}}>\underset{\text { (II) }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{\ominus}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Which among the following halides will not show ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1}}$ reaction?
(I) ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}}}$
(II) ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{Cl}}}$
(III) (IV) Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
explanation:
Carbocation intermidiates are formed during ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1}}$ mechanism.
(I) forms stable allyl carbocation ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_{2}}}$,
(II) forms highly unstable vinyl carbocation ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}}}$,
(III)forms benzyl carbocation https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954673/preptracker/questions/q_27482_explanation_1764954672666.png which is stable due to resonance, and
(IV) forms secondary carbocation ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}}}$.
Hence, (${\text{II}}$) will not show ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1}}$ reaction. So, the correct options is (2) .

question: The correct statement regarding nucleophilic substitution reaction in a chiral alkyl halide is
explanation:
As ${\mathrm{S_{N} 1}}$ reaction proceeds via the formation of a planar carbocation intermediate, it allows attack by nucleophile from either direction leading to racemisation.
On the other hand, in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2}}$ reaction attack by nucleophile occurs from the backside leading to inversion of configuration of the chiral alkyl halide. So, the correct options is (4) .

options.text: retention occurs in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1}}$ reaction and inversion occurs in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2}}$ reaction
racemisation occurs in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1}}$ reaction and retention occurs in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2}}$ reaction
racemisation occurs in both ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2}}$ reactions
racemisation occurs in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1}}$ reaction and inversion occurs in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2}}$ reaction.

explanation: KCN is predominantly ionic and provides cyanide ions in solution. question: A partially racemised ${\mathrm{(+)}}$-2-bromooctane ${\mathrm{\left(2^{\circ} \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X}\right)}}$ on reaction with aq. NaOH in acetone gives an alcohol with ${\mathrm{80 \%}}$ inversion and ${\mathrm{20 \%}}$ racemisation, the ratio of percentage of front side attack to back side attack is ____ .
explanation:
In ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2}}$ reaction when nucleophile attacks from backside the stereochemistry of product will change and there is inversion in configuration. ${\mathrm{20 \%}}$ racemisation occurs, out of which ${\text{10}}\,{\text{% }}$ is inversion product and rest of the product is ${\mathrm{100-20=80 \%}}$
$\therefore {\rm{\% }}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{back}}\,{\rm{side}}\,{\rm{attack}}\,{\rm{ = 80 + 10}} = {\rm{90\% }}$
In front side attack stereochemistry of product remains same i.e. retention of configuration. ${\mathrm{20 \%}}$ racemic product has ${\mathrm{10 \%}}$retention
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ percentage of front side attack ${\mathrm{=10 \%}}$
$\text { Ratio }=10 \% \div 90 \%=0.11$

question: Which of the following is not correct?
explanation:
In $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reactions, carbocations are the intermediates. Greater the stability of carbocation, more easily it is formed and, hence faster will be the rate of reaction.
The stability of carbocation decreases in the order:
${{\rm{3}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{carbocation}}\,{\rm{ > }}{{\rm{2}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{carbocation}}\,{\rm{ > }}{{\rm{1}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{carbocation}}{\rm{.}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{ph}}\,\mathop {{\rm{C(}}}\limits^{\rm{ + }} {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{{\rm{)}}_{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{ > }}\,{\rm{ph}}\,\mathop {{\rm{CH}}}\limits^{\rm{ + }} \,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{3}}\,}}{\rm{ > }}\,{\rm{ph}}\mathop {\,{\rm{C}}}\limits^{\rm{ + }} {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ $
Thus, option (1) is incorrect, while trends in other options are correct.

explanation:
The order of reactivity of alkyl halides towards $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ reactions is $1^{\circ}>2^{\circ}>3^{\circ}$. The order of reactivity of alkyl halides towards $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reactions is $3^{\circ}>2^{\circ}>1^{\circ}$.
$ \bullet $ In (A), https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959025/preptracker/questions/q_26853_explanation_1764959024969.png is a $1^{\circ}$ alkyl halide, whereas https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959026/preptracker/questions/q_26853_explanation_1764959025885.png is a $3^{\circ}$ alkyl halide. Thus the order is correct.
$ \bullet $ In (B), https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959026/preptracker/questions/q_26853_explanation_1764959026487.png forms a benzylic carbocation. It is more stable and hence the given order is correct.
$ \bullet $ In option (C), which shows electrophilic substitution, in https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959027/preptracker/questions/q_26853_explanation_1764959027147.png the electron density will be more as compared to https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959028/preptracker/questions/q_26853_explanation_1764959027905.png because of strong $ - \,{\text{I}}$ and $ - \,{\text{R}}$ effects of $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group, hence the given order of reactivity is correct.
$ \bullet $ In option (D) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959029/preptracker/questions/q_26853_explanation_1764959028665.png is less electron rich than https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959030/preptracker/questions/q_26853_explanation_1764959029848.png due to strong -I and -R effects of $ - {\rm{N}}{{\rm{O}}_2}$ group. question:
The graph which represents the following reaction is
\(\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl} \xrightarrow[\text { Pyridine }]{\mathrm{OH}^{-}}\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{OH}\)
explanation:
$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ mechanism:
Step 1 : $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{CCl} \xrightarrow[\text { Slow step }]{-\mathrm{Cl}^{-}} \underset{\substack{\text { Carbocation } \\ \text { (Stable) }}}{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}^{+}}$
Step 2 :$\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}^{+}+\overline{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{H} \xrightarrow[\text { Fast step }]{ }\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{OH}$
Slow step is always a rate determining step.
Hence, rate $\propto\left[\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{CCl}\right]$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958984/preptracker/questions/q_26838_explanation_1764958984176.png
Overall reaction rate is directly proportional to the concentration of reactant, in the rate determining step.

question:

Reason: The reaction occurs by $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ mechanism.
explanation: Optically active alkyl halides undergoing ${{\text{S}}_{\text{N}}}{\text{1}}$  reactions often lead to racemization. This process occurs due to the formation of a planar, $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$-hybridized carbocation intermediate. The nucleophilic attack can occur from either side of this planar intermediate, resulting in a mixture of products with opposite configurations  yielding a racemic mixture as the final outcome. So option (1) is correct.

Reason: $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ reactions always proceed with inversion of configuration.
explanation:
In an $S_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ reaction involving an optically active alkyl halide and an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{KOH}$, the nucleophile attacks from the opposite side and there is inversion of configuration. As a result,the product formed is the enantiomer of the starting optically active alkyl halide, leading to an alcohol with the opposite optical rotation.
So option (1) is correct.

explanation: The carbocation i.ntermediate derived from $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right) \mathrm{Br}$ is more stable than that from $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right) \mathrm{Br}$ because of the stabilization conferred by two phenyl groups through resonance. Consequently, the former is more reactive than the latter in $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reactions due to the enhanced stability of its carbocation intermediate. Thus option (3) is correct.

As the size of the alkyl groups inereases, the $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ reactivity decreases, further $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond is stronger and more difficult to cleave than
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Br}$ bond. So $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Br}$ is more reactive than $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CHCl} . \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ reactions are always accompanied by inversion of configuration as the incoming nucleophile attacks from the rare side even before the leaving group is removed. So option (2) is correct.

The $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Br}$ bond possesses a bond dissociation energy of $1293 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$, whereas the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond has a higher bond dissociation energy of $1351 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. Reason: Silver nitrite $\left(\mathrm{AgNO}_{2}\right)$ is an ionic compound, therefore the negative charge on nitrogen is the attacking site.
explanation: Silver nitrite is primarily a covalent compound, and the $\mathrm{Ag}-\mathrm{O}$ bond within it holds a covalent character. Consequently, there is no negative charge present on the oxygen atom within silver nitrite. As a result, the nucleophilic attack takes place via the lone pair situated on nitrogen, leading to the formation of nitroalkanes $\left(\mathrm{RNO}_{2}\right)$. So option (3) is correct.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Vinyl chloride is less reactive than alkyl chloride.


Reason: Stability of alkyl halide decreases as the strength of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond decreases.
explanation: Vinyl chloride is resonance stabilized. As such the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond exhibits a partial double bond character due to which it is difficult to break it. This makes vinyl chloride less reactive than alkyl halides.Weaker the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond, less stable the alkyl halide. So option (2) is correct.

\[\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\rm{Reactants}}}&{{\rm{Products}}}\\{{\rm{I}}{\rm{.}}\,\,{\rm{RX}}\,{\rm{ + }}\,{\rm{AgO}}{{\rm{H}}_{\left( {{\rm{aq}}} \right)}}}&{{\rm{RH}}}\\{{\rm{II}}{\rm{.}}\,\,{\rm{RX}}\,{\rm{ + }}\,{\rm{AgC}}{{\rm{N}}_{\left( {{\rm{alc}}} \right)}}}&{{\rm{RNC}}}\\{{\rm{III}}{\rm{.}}\,\,{\rm{RX}}\,{\rm{ + }}\,{\rm{KC}}{{\rm{N}}_{\left( {{\rm{alc}}} \right)}}}&{{\rm{RNC}}}\\{{\rm{IV}}{\rm{.}}\,\,{\rm{RX}}\,{\rm{ + }}\,{\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\left( {{\rm{ether}}} \right)}}}&{{\rm{R - R}}}\end{array}\]

II and IV reactions are correctly matched with its products.

question: The order of reactivity of the given haloalkanes towards nucleophile is:
explanation:
For a given alkyl group, the order of reactivity is
$$\dfrac{\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{I}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{F}}{\text { Decreasing bond energy }}$$
This order depends on the carbon-halogen bond energy; the carbon-fluorine bond energy is maximum and thus fluorides are least reactive while carbon-iodine bond energy is minimum hence iodides are most reactive.

question: Reaction of $$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}$$ with aqueous sodium hydroxide follows
explanation:
$$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}{ }^{1}$$ mechanism depends upon the stability of carbocation. Higher the stability of carbocation, higher will be the possibility of $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}{ }^{1}$$ mechanism to take place. In the given compound $$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}$$, carbocation is $$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_{2}$$. This carbocation
$$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_{2}$$ is a stable carbocation due to resonance, therefore, it shows $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}{ }^{1}$$ mechanism.https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_08_18_38724f468cb258312af5g-17.jpg?height=440&width=528&top_left_y=654&top_left_x=300

options.text: Any of the above two depending upon the temperature of reaction

question:
In ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}{\rm{2}}$ reactions, the correct order of reactivity for the following compounds:
$$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl},\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CHCl}$$ and $$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{CCl}$$ is:
explanation:
Steric hindrance around the carbon atom undergoing the inversion process will slow down the ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}{\rm{2}}$ reaction, hence less hindrance faster will the reaction. So, the order is
$$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}>\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CHCl}>\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{CCl}$$
subject: Chemistry

explanation:
For any ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}^{\rm{1}}$ reaction is decided by ease of dissociation of alkyl halide
$$\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{X} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{R}^{\oplus}+\mathrm{X}^{\odot}$$
Higher the stability of $$R^{\oplus}$$ (carbocation) higher would be reactivity of $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}{ }^{1}$$ reaction.
Since stability of cation follows order.
$$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_{2} < \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \\& < \mathrm{p}-\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{4}-\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_{2}\end{aligned}$$
Hence correct order is
II $$ < $$ I $$ < $$ III

(iii) $$\mathrm{MeCl}$$
(iv) $$\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{MeOC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{Br}$$, towords ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}$ reactions decreases as
explanation:

(iii) $$\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{MeOC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{Br}$$ (iv), i.e., option (4) is correct.

question: Which of the following statements is correct?
explanation:
In case of optically active alkyl halides, the product formed as a result of ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}^1$ mechanism has the inverted configuration as compared to the reactant. This is because the nucleophile attaches itself on the side opposite to the one where the halogen atom is present.
In case of optically active alkyl halides, ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}^1$ reactions are accompanied by racemisation. The carbocation formed in the slow step being ${\rm{s}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{2}}}$ hybridised is planar (achiral).

options.text: ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}{\rm{2}}$ reactions of optically active halides are accompained by inversion of configuration.
${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}{\rm{1}}$ reactions of optically active halides are accompained by racemisation.
Carbocation formed in ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}{\rm{1}}$ reaction is ${\rm{s}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{2}}}$ hybridized.

question:
Consider the reactions:
(i) ${\left( {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}{\text{CH - C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{Br}}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}{\text{OH}}}}$
$$(\mathrm{CH})_{2} \mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+\mathrm{HBr}$$
(ii) ${\left( {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}{\text{CH - C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{Br}}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}{{\text{O}}^{\text{ - }}}}}$
$$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+\mathrm{Br}^{-}$$
The mechanisms of reactions (i) and (ii) are respectively:
explanation:
A strong nucleophile favours the ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}^2$ reaction and a weak nucleophile favours the ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}^1$ reaction. First reaction is ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}^1$ reaction because $$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$$ is used as solvent which is a weak nucleophile.
Second reaction is ${{\rm{S}}_{\rm{N}}}^2$ reaction because $$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{O}^{-}$$is strong nucleophile.

${{\text{EtN}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + MeMgI}}\xrightarrow[{{\text{ temp}}{\text{. with pyridine }}}]{{{\text{Heated}}{\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\text{at}}{\kern 1pt} {\text{high}}}}{\text{Gas(A)}}}$
The volume of gas (A) obtained at STP when
0.45 g of ${{\rm{EtN}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}}$ reacts with ${\text{ MeMgI}}$ is ____ .
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957283/preptracker/questions/q_27299_explanation_1764957283271.png One mole of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Et}-\mathrm{NH}_{2}}}$ reacts with 2 moles of MeMgI to give 2 mol . of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})}}$

So, ${\mathrm{45 \mathrm{~g} \,\mathrm{Et}-\mathrm{NH}_{2}}}$ gives ${\mathrm{2 \times 22.4 \mathrm{~L}}}$ of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}}$ at STP
$0.45\,{\text{g of Et}} - {\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{ gives C}}{{\text{H}}_4}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \frac{{2 \times 22.4 \times 0.45}}{{45}} = 0.448\,{\text{L}} = 0.45\,{\text{L}}$

explanation:

question:
The arrangement of following compounds:
(i) bromomethane (ii) bromoform
(iii) chloromethane (iv) dibromomethane
In the increasing order of their boiling point is
explanation:
The intermolecular van der Waal's forces of attraction became stronger with the increase in size of the molecules. Hence the order of boiling points will be:
$\begin{gathered} {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{Cl}} < {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{Br}} < {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{B}}{{\text{r}}_2} < {\text{CHB}}{{\text{r}}_3} \hfill \\ \,{\text{(iii)}}\quad \quad {\text{(i)}}\quad \quad \quad {\text{(iv)}}\quad \quad \quad {\text{(ii)}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} $

question: Match the physical properties listed in Column I with their corresponding effects or observations in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given
explanation:
A - Q : The size of the molecule, which includes the molecular mass and number of electrons, affects the boiling point due to the magnitude of van der Waals forces.
B - P: Polarity of haloalkanes contributes to their higher boiling points compared to those of similar hydrocarbons.
C - S: Increased branching in a molecule leads to a decrease in boiling point because of reduced surface area and thus reduced van der Waals forces.
D - R: The presence of halogen atoms increases the density and often the boiling points of haloalkanes due to the added mass and intermolecular attractions. Correct option is (1)

options.text: A - Q,       	B - P, 	C - S, 	D - R
A - Q, 	B - P,	C - R,	D - S
A - R, 	B - S, 	C - P, 	D - Q
A - S,  	B - R,	C - Q,	D - P

Methyl chloride, methyl bromide, and some chlorofluoromethanes are gases at room temperature.
The larger alkyl halides with higher molecular weights are typically liquids at room temperature.
explanation: Statement  A  is true as smaller alkyl halides like methyl chloride and methyl bromide are indeed gases at room temperature. question:

Alkyl halides are very slightly soluble in water.
Alkyl halides are highly soluble in organic solvents due to their non-polar nature.
explanation: Statement  A  is true because alkyl halides are slightly soluble in water as the energy required to disrupt the hydrogen bonding in water and intermolecular forces in the alkyl halides is not fully compensated by the formation of new interactions. Statement B is also true, as alkyl halides are soluble in organic solvents, where intermolecular forces similar in strength to those being disrupted are formed between solvent molecules and the halides. Correct option is (1)

question: Increasing order of dipole moment of following compounds
explanation:
Dipole moment $\propto$ polarity
$\propto \dfrac{1}{\text { Bond angle }}$
$\propto \dfrac{1}{\text { Symmetry }}$
Due to symmetry (iv) possess least dipole moment. Based on bond angle, the dipole moment of (ii) is greater than (iii).
Based on polarity, the dipole moment of (i) is less than (ii) & (iii).
Hence, the increasing order of dipole moment is (iv) < (i) < (ii) < (iii).

Reason: Stronger the van der Waals' forces of attraction, higher is the melting point.
explanation: The para-isomer of dichlorobenzene being symmetrical exhibits a closed packing arrangement within the crystal lattice. Consequently, it possesses a much higher melting point compared to the $\mathrm{o}$ - and $\mathrm{m}$ - isomers melting point depends on Vander waal's forces also. So option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: n-Butyl bromide has higher boiling point than isobutyl bromide.


Reason: The branching of the chain makes the molecule more compact and therefore decreases the surface area.
explanation: Branches in a molecular chain results in a more compact structure, reducing the overall surface area. This reduction in surface area diminishes the strength of van der Waals forces of attraction. Consequently, the boiling points of branched chain compounds tend to be lower than those of straight-chain compounds due to the decreased magnitude of these intermolecular forces. So option (1) is correct.

Reason: p-dichlorobenzenc possesses low melting point.
explanation:
Haloalkanes dissolve in organic solvents because the new intermolecular attractions between haloalkanes and solvent molecules have much the same strength as the ones being broken in the separate haloalkane and solvent molecules.
The high melting point of p-dichlorobenzene can be attributed to its symmetrical nature and compact, close packing. Thus, more energy is needed to break apart the molecules. So option (3) is correct.

question: Match the polyhalogen compounds in Column I with their respective uses in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
Dichloromethane (Methylene chloride) is widely used as a solvent and is particularly used as a paint remover due to its solvent properties, thus A matches with S.
Trichloromethane (Chloroform) was used as a general anesthetic in surgeries before being replaced by safer alternatives, thus B matches with R.
Triiodomethane (Iodoform) was used for its antiseptic properties which are due to the liberation of free iodine, though it is not commonly used now due to its objectionable smell, thus C matches with Q .
Tetrachloromethane (Carbon tetrachloride) is used in the manufacture of refrigerants, making D match with P. Correct option is (2)

${\rm{RN}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + }}\,{\rm{alc}}{\rm{.KOH}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ + }}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{CHC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{3}}} \to \mathop {{\rm{RNC}}}\limits_{{\rm{Carbylamine}}} $

question:

explanation: Chloroform is heavier than water. It is acidic due to the easy release of the $\mathrm{H}$ atom as $\mathrm{H}^{+}$. Therefore, option (3) is correct.

question:
Which of the following halides undergoes
hydrolysis on warming with water/aqueous NaOH ?
explanation: Chlorobenzene does not react with aqueous NaOH at room temperature but presence of electron withdrawing groups like $-\mathrm{NO}_{2},-\mathrm{CN}$, at ortho or para position enhance the reactivity of aryl halides towards nucleophilic substitution.  Further,  greater the number of such groups at $\mathrm{o}-$ and p $ - $ position w.r.t. the halogen, more reactive is the haloarene.   Hence, 1- chloro-2,  4,  6 - trinitrobenzene will be hydrolysed the most on warming with water/aqueous NaOH .
subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Aryl halides can undergo a nucleophilic substitution reaction when there is a strong electron-withdrawing group in the ortho or para position.
The presence of an electron donating group in the aryl halide enhances its reactivity towards nucleophilic substitution.
explanation: Statement  A  is true as the presence of strong electron-withdrawing groups in the ortho or para position activates the aryl halide towards nucleophilic substitution by stabilizing the negative charge in the intermediate. Statement B is false because electron-donating groups would decrease the reactivity towards nucleophilic substitution. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Aryl halides are more reactive than alkyl halides in nucleophilic substitution reactions.
The carbon-halogen bond in aryl halides has partial double-bond character due to resonance, which makes the bond stronger and less reactive.
explanation: Aryl halides are less reactive than alkyl halides in nucleophilic substitution reactions due to the partial double-bond character of the carbon-halogen bond, which is correctly  explained in Statement B. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Aryl halides react with potassium fluoride to produce aryl fluorides via a nucleophilic aromatic substitution reaction.
Aryl halides are less reactive than alkyl halides towards nucleophilic substitution due to the partial double bond character of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond in aryl halides.
explanation: Statement  A  is false because aryl halides do not easily undergo nucleophilic aromatic substitution due to the stability of the aromatic system and the partial double bond character of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond. Statement B is true because the partial double bond character in aryl halides does indeed make them less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution as compared to alkyl halides. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: Chlorobenzene is less reactive than cyclohexyl chloride towards hydrolysis.

Reason: The $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond in chlorobenzene has partial double-bond character due to resonance stabilization, making it difficult to hydrolyze.
explanation: Chlorobenzene is indeed less reactive towards hydrolysis than cyclohexyl chloride due to the delocalization of electrons in the aromatic ring which imparts partial double-bond character to the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond, thus making it less susceptible to hydrolysis. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question: Order of reactivity towards nucleophilic substitution reaction of the compounds
explanation:
Nucleophile will preferentially attack on the compound which has lower electron density. Out of the given options, (IV) has four electron withdrawing groups and is the most preferred substrate.As, the number of electrons withdrawing groups decreases or the number of electron donating groups increases, the electron density of the compound increases. Hence, the reactivity towards nucleophilic substitution reaction decreases.So, the order is
(IV) > (III) > (II) > (I)

question:
Read the statements given below.
(A) Haloarenes require drastic conditions for nucleophilic substitution reaction (NSR).
(B) Presence of electron withdrawing group increases the reactivity of haloarenes towards NSR.
(C) Electrophilic substitution of haloarenes is slower than benzene.
Choose the option containing correct set of statements.
explanation:
All the statements are correct.Haloarenes are very less rective towards NSR due to double bond nature of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}}}$ bond. But EWG increases the reactivity towards NSR.Reactivity towards NSR increases by ${\mathrm{-\mathrm{NO}_{2}}}$ (EWG). Also, electrophilic substitution of haloarene is slower than benzene as halogen ${\mathrm{(-\mathrm{I}>+\mathrm{M})}}$ decreases the electron density of benzene.
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Chemistry

question:
Aryl halides are extremely less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution than alkyl halides.
Which of the following accounts for this?
(I) Due to resonance in aryl halides.
(II) In alkyl halides, carbon atom in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}}}$ bond is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{sp}^{2}}}$ hybridized whereas in aryl halides carbon atom in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}}}$ bond is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{sp}^{3}}}$ hybridized.
(III) Due to stability of phenyl cation.
(IV)Due to possible repulsion there are less chances of nucleophile to approach electron rich arenes.
explanation: (ii) and (iii) are incorrect as carbon of alkyl halide and aryl halide is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{sp}^{3}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{sp}^{2}}}$ hybridized, respectively. Also phenyl cation is highly unstable. Aryl halides are less reactive towards SN reactions due to resonance and possible repulsion there are less chances of nucleophile to approach ${\mathrm{\mathrm{e}^{-}}}$ rich arenes.
subject: Chemistry

question: The major product formed in the Friedel-Craft acylation of chlorobenzene is
